skill.name: analyzing-patterns
skill.description: Analyze software codebases to identify design patterns, architecture patterns, repeated structures, conventions, and anti-patterns.
skill.summary: Find recurring code and architecture patterns in software projects.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 343 issues and a 100/100 risk score, but review shows most alerts are markdown examples, pattern keywords, or safe documentation references. The skill is not blocked because no malicious intent, prompt injection, credential access, or data exfiltration was found; it should publish with a warning because it includes local scanning scripts and shell command recipes.

medium_findings.title: Local Recursive Codebase Scanning Script
medium_findings.description: The Python helper accepts a directory argument, resolves it, recursively reads matching source files, and prints discovered pattern locations. This is legitimate for pattern analysis, but users should run it only on repositories they intend to inspect because it reads local file contents and paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly traverses a user-supplied directory and reads files. No network transfer or credential collection was found, so the risk is local exposure rather than exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Command-Line Analysis Recipes
medium_findings.description: The skill includes grep, find, tree, python, and bash examples for codebase inspection. These commands are related to the skill purpose, but marketplace users should review command scope before running them on sensitive repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit documentation examples and analysis recipes, not hidden execution. They still create operational risk if copied into a shell without review.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many external command, weak cryptography, network, database, and reconnaissance alerts are triggered by markdown examples that describe software patterns. I found no evidence that these examples execute automatically, transmit data, or collect credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are fenced examples or reference tables. Their context is explanatory documentation, and no automatic execution path was found.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Require Alert Is Descriptive Text
low_findings.description: The dynamic require finding points to a bullet that names CommonJS module syntax. It is not executable code and does not instruct dynamic loading from user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a module-pattern description inside a markdown list. There is no JavaScript file or runtime call associated with it.

low_findings.title: Network Alert Is Pattern Documentation
low_findings.description: The network finding maps to pattern catalog material about application design and sample frontend/API code. No real endpoint, request library import, or data exfiltration path was found in executable files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious text appears in TypeScript examples used to explain design patterns. The only executable script is local file analysis and does not import a network client.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Recursive Codebase Scanning Script
dangerous_patterns.description: The Python helper accepts a directory argument, resolves it, recursively reads matching source files, and prints discovered pattern locations. This is legitimate for pattern analysis, but users should run it only on repositories they intend to inspect because it reads local file contents and paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly traverses a user-supplied directory and reads files. No network transfer or credential collection was found, so the risk is local exposure rather than exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command-Line Analysis Recipes
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes grep, find, tree, python, and bash examples for codebase inspection. These commands are related to the skill purpose, but marketplace users should review command scope before running them on sensitive repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit documentation examples and analysis recipes, not hidden execution. They still create operational risk if copied into a shell without review.

content.value_statement: Large codebases hide repeated structures, architecture choices, and anti-patterns. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured workflow for finding and explaining those patterns.
actual_capabilities: Identifies common design patterns such as factory, observer, strategy, repository, builder, adapter, and command.
Maps architecture styles including MVC, layered architecture, microservices, event-driven systems, and hexagonal architecture.
Finds repeated code structures, naming conventions, file organization patterns, import styles, and module boundaries.
Highlights anti-patterns such as god objects, copy-paste programming, magic values, tight coupling, and circular dependencies.
Provides structured reports with locations, purpose, implementation quality, and practical refactoring recommendations.
Includes a local Python helper for detecting pattern signatures in common source file types.
limitations: It relies on code inspection and heuristics, so results need developer review before refactoring.
The helper script detects signatures, not full semantic intent or runtime behavior.
Shell examples should be reviewed before use on large, private, or sensitive repositories.
It does not replace architecture decision records, threat modeling, or full static analysis tools.

use_cases.title: Map a New Codebase
use_cases.description: Quickly summarize architecture layers, recurring modules, and dominant implementation patterns during onboarding or acquisition review.

use_cases.title: Plan Refactoring Work
use_cases.description: Find repeated code structures, inconsistent conventions, and anti-patterns before extracting shared utilities or services.

use_cases.title: Improve Code Reviews
use_cases.description: Ask for pattern-focused review notes that explain whether a design choice is consistent, useful, or overcomplicated.

prompt_templates.title: Find Main Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this repository for the main design and architecture patterns. Summarize the patterns, where they appear, and why they matter.

prompt_templates.title: Review Repeated Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Find repeated code structures and naming conventions in this project. Group similar examples and suggest practical refactoring options.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Architecture Quality
prompt_templates.scenario: Architecture review for a product area
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the application architecture in this codebase. Identify layers, data flow, boundaries, strengths, weaknesses, and important tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritize Pattern Refactors
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a pattern and anti-pattern audit for this codebase. Prioritize findings by impact, confidence, effort, and expected maintenance benefit.

output_examples.input: Analyze the architecture of this web application.
output_examples.output: The response groups the application into presentation, service, data access, and shared utility layers. It explains data flow, boundary issues, and recommended improvements.

output_examples.input: Find repeated code patterns in the user management module.
output_examples.output: The response lists repeated validation, data fetching, and mapping patterns. It separates acceptable consistency from duplication that should become shared helpers.

output_examples.input: What design patterns are used in this repository?
output_examples.output: The response identifies patterns such as repository, factory, observer, and strategy. Each item includes purpose, locations, quality notes, and refactoring guidance.

best_practices: Start with a small folder or module before expanding the analysis to the whole repository.
Ask for evidence with file and line references so each pattern can be checked by a developer.
Treat recommendations as design input, then validate them with tests, profiling, and team context.
anti_patterns: Do not refactor every repeated shape without checking whether the repetition is useful consistency.
Do not assume a pattern name proves good design; evaluate fit, coupling, and maintainability.
Do not run command examples on sensitive repositories without reviewing scope and output first.

faq.question: What does this skill analyze?
faq.answer: It analyzes software structure, design patterns, architecture patterns, repeated code, conventions, and common anti-patterns.

faq.question: Does it change my code?
faq.answer: No. The skill focuses on inspection and reporting. Any refactoring still requires a separate implementation step.

faq.question: Can it detect every design pattern?
faq.answer: No. It combines heuristics and code review guidance, so subtle patterns may need human confirmation.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The skill includes local scanning scripts and shell command examples. These are useful, but users should review scope before running them.

faq.question: Does it send code to external services?
faq.answer: No evidence of network transfer was found in the reviewed files. The included script reads local files and prints a local report.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: Developers, reviewers, and engineering leads can use it to understand code organization and plan maintainable refactoring work.
